title: U.S. Ground Troops Coming to Iran || Peter Zeihan

transcript.text: Hey y'all, Peter Zion here coming to you
March 25th from inside the house in
Colorado because uh false alarm. I'm not
getting better. I'm sicker. Anyway,
going to talk about uh Iran today,
specifically what's going on with ground
troops, which looks like they're
absolutely going to be used. Um this is
a bad idea from any number of manners,
but let me give you an idea of what's in
play and what it might be used for, and
we'll go from there.
So, uh, as you may remember, the USS
Tripoli, that's one of America's
amphibious assault carriers, carries a
clutch of F-35 fighter jets, as well as
2,00 to 2500 Marines. Um, relocated from
the Philippine Sea and is sailing
through the Indian Ocean. Now, it will
probably arrive in the vicinity of the
Persian Gulf in the next day or two. We
also have the USS Boxer, which is
another MEU, Marine Expeditionary Unit.
That's basically what the triple E is.
Uh which has left San Diego, uh it will
not be in area for probably close to 3
weeks. But the the news from yesterday
is that the 82nd Airborne, which is kind
of America's rapid reaction force, uh
troops that are run by the army that are
mated with permanent transport aircraft
that can deploy to anywhere in the world
in less than 48 hours. They were given
their marching orders to move to the
Middle East uh last night. Uh and will
probably well they could be moving right
now. Um you know, all hush hush
classified, but when the uh orders are
given, it's usually not too long until
they're on their way. Uh collectively in
3 weeks, this means we have about 8,000
troops in the region and they're really
heavy hitters. Basically, unless you're
going to move into special operations,
the Marines and the airborne are about
as good as they get without having
armor. That's something else that's
important to note. So, this is not a
traditional ground invasion where we're
driving tanks and using artillery. This
would be a relatively light force that
punches above its weight for what it is,
but is not designed to take on another
major force. Uh, keep in mind that the
Iranians don't have another major force.
So, that should be fine in that regard.
They may have a million man army, but
it's primarily designed to shoot
civilians rather than swarm over foreign
countries.
Now, uh, the news is going on and on and
on and on about something called Car
Island. As as someone has highlighted
Car Island 15 years ago, part of me is
like, "Yay, finally." Uh, but I really
doubt that's the target. Or more to the
point, I really doubt that that should
be the target. Car Island is a small
facility off the western coast of
southwestern Iran. It's up in the
northern Gulf. It doesn't have a bridge
to it. Uh, it was built by foreigners
and has a subsea pipeline that basically
carries all Persian Gulf crude to it
because the Persian Gulf Coast is
really, really bad to accept tankers.
It's just very shallow and muddy.
So, they have this island out where they
can accept tankers. And it is the point
for 90% plus of Iran's oil exports. And
what we've seen in the war so far is
that the United States came in
completely unprepared for the idea that
something might happen to the Persian
Gulf and really didn't have the hardware
or the positioning in place to protect
ships in the Gulf or make sure that the
Iranians couldn't attack ships in the
Gulf. Uh so everyone's now focusing on
Carg and the logic seems to be that
Donald Trump wants to make another
deal. And the idea is that if
the United States occupies Car Island,
then Trump will have a negotiating card
to play against Iran elsewhere. So we
will give you Car Island back if you
stop attacking places in the Gulf,
specifically if you allow the Straight
of Hormuz to uh be open. Um, I will tell
you bluntly and anyone who studies the
Middle East will tell you bluntly and uh
that that will not work uh because that
will remove Iran's oil income and then
the gloves will really come off. And as
we've seen when the Israelis attacked a
natural gas processing facility last
week, the Iranians had more than enough
weapons left to open up on
infrastructure at the Gulf and they did
well over hundred billion dollars of
damage in a matter of hours. uh you
would probably get something like that.
In addition, uh Carg Island uh is not
going to be easy to defend. One of the
things the United States has been doing
is its carriers haven't even come in the
Gulf. One has been off in the Arabian
Sea. The other one has been off in the
Red Sea. They've been fighting Iran at
distance. If you're going to put a few
thousand troops in Carg, you're going to
need close in support. and it is within
30 mi of the coast and the Iranians will
hit it with everything they have because
the ability to hit several thousand
American troops with limited defenses
right up and close. Oh man, they've been
waiting for a situation like that the
entire war. It would basically be
putting them in the most vulnerable way
you could imagine. Now, like I said,
anyone who knows anything about this
region or oil politics or how defenses
work would have told the president that
this isn't how things go. But the
president isn't being told anything. uh
the people in the department of energy
and the DoD who were responsible for
studying things like choke points and um
the straight of horm specifically were
all fired last year and the same goes
for basically any sort of strategic
planning or think work. Uh Pete Hegsth,
the Secretary of Defense, has been going
on a crusade in his anything that does
not actively support the war fighter is
being cut out of the Department of the
Defense. And that removes all education
that would allow people to make educated
decisions about say what you do in war.
So when I say that this is a Donald
Trump plan, this is a Donald Trump plan.
uh he's no longer allowing information
to reach him with the possible exception
of through Dean Kaine who is joint
chiefs of staff uh and that's about the
only voice of caution he's get and so
far he has overruled Kane on really
everything of substance so if this is
where we're going this is going to end
in a bit of a debacle uh the only other
thing that I can think of where 8,000
troops that are heavy on the amphibious
component might be of use is in the
straight of Hormuz itself if you put uh
marines and airborne in that area and
enable them to do land strikes uh rapid
and then retreat land strikes up and
down the straight of Hormuz you can
probably limit the ability of Iran to
launch attacks on civilian tankers. And
since the United States doesn't have the
ships, doesn't have the hardware to do a
meaningful convoy system, this might be
the next best plan. It's not a great
one, but I would find it much more
viable than say going after Kar. That's
my two cents. Anyway, that's where we
are right now. Um, the Tripoli will
arrive within 48 hours and the 82nd
could be there at the same time. So, if
you only feel you need 2/3 of these
forces, you could get going on it right
away. If you decide you need the full
8,000, you have to wait for the boxer to
arrive. And that will not be until the
second week of April. Anyway, uh pieces
are moving so that these options are
available. Whether they are used, of
course, depends upon uh what Trump feels
because that's all that matters in this
war.
